enfo~nt 
322-6-4041 
Re:~ Your Request for Advice 
Our Advice No. A-84-187 
Dear Mr. Newman: 
Thank you for your request for advice on the disclosure 
provisions of the Politica~ Reform Act. 
FACTS 
You are an associate consultant to the Assembly Committee on 
Economic Development and New Technologies. 
Recently, an 
industry association held an informational conference. 
Association members were charged $150 and industry 
representatives who were not association members were charged 
$~75. Because the information presented at the conference was 
relevant to legislative research that you are conducting, you 
were allowed to attend the conference free of charge. 
No 
specific legislation was discussed at the conference. 
You paid 
your own travel costs. 
QUESTION PRESENTED 
Do the disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act 
require you to disclose, as a ~ift, the free admission to the 
conference? 
CONCLUSION 
You are not required to disclose the free admission as a 
gift. 


Joshua Newman 
August 14 r 1984 
Page- 2 
,-
DISCUSSION 
Under the provisions of the Political Reform Act gifts of 
$50 or more must be disclosed on a public official's!1 
Statement of Economic Interests~1 (Government Code Section 
8720711). 
However, the term "gift" does not include "infor-
mational material such as books, reports, pamphlets, calendars 
or periodicals" (Section 82028). 
This provision has been 
interpreted to exclude from disclosure free admission to 
informational tours. 
Similarly, the staff feels that free 
admission to informational conferences should also be 
excluded • ..!1 
Section 82028(b) (2) states that payment for travel or 
reimbursement for any expenses shall not be included in the 
informational material exemption.i1 
In the present case, you 
paid your own travel expenses and this provision has no 
application. 
If r can be' of any additional help to you, please feel free 
to contact me at 916/322-5901. 
JSM:km 
Very truly yours, 
:t1UUiJ 8~ 
~&2.M/ 
Janis Shank McLean 
Counsel, Legal Division 
1/ The term "public official" includes employees and 
consultants of state agencies (Government Code Section 82048). 
~I If the disclosure is required by the official's 
disclosure category. 
11 Hereinafter all statutory references made are to the 
Government Code unless otherwise indicated. 
il If, in the future, you are provided with free 
admission to an informational conference which is intended to 
address a specific piece of legislation, please contact the FPPC 
again, as this may affect the application of this exemption. 
11 
However, other provisions may exempt payment for 
travel or reimbursement for expenses from disclosure. 
(See 
Section 82030(b) (2) and 2 cal. Adm. Code Section 18728, copy 
enclosed. )

Dear Mr. Newman: 
Thank you for your request for advice on the disclosure 
provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
FACTS 
You are an associate consultant to the Assembly Committee on 
Economic Development and New Technologies. 
Recently, an 
industry association held an informational conference. 
Association members were charged $150 and industry 
representatives who were not association members were charged 
$175. 
Because the information presented at the conference was 
relevant to legislative research that you are conducting, you 
were allowed to attend the conference free of charge. 
No 
specific legislation was discussed at the conference. 
You paid 
your own travel costs. 
QUESTION PRESENTED 
Do the disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act 
require you to disclose, as a gift, the free admission to the 
conference? 
CONCLUSION 
You are not required to disclose the free admission as a 
gift. 


Joshua Newman 
August 14, 1984 
Page 2 
DISCUSSION 
Under the provisions of the Political Reform Acti gifts of 
$50 or more must be disclosed on a public official's_/ 
Statement of Economic Interests1/ (Government Code Section 
872071/). 
However, the term "gift" does not include "infor-
mational material such as books, reports, pamphlets, calendars 
or periodicals" (Section 82028). 
This provision has been 
interpreted to exclude from disclosure free admission to 
informational tours. 
Similarly, the staff feels that free 
admission to informational conferences should also be 
excluded • .,i/ 
Section 82028(b) (2) states that payment for travel or 
reimbursement for any expenses shall not be included in the 
informational material exemption.2/ 
In the present case, you 
paid your own travel expenses and this provision has no 
application. 
If I can be of any additional help to you, please feel free 
to contact me at 916/322-5901. 
JSM:km 
Very truly yours, 
.jNU1J 8!iAJ, ~~

Fair PoliticaL Practices Commission 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 95809 
Dear Ms. 
lman: 
At the suggestion of Carla Wardlow of your Technical 
Assistance Division, I am writing 
clarification of whether 
the waiver of a conference fee constitutes a reportable" 
ft" 
under the Fair Political Practice Act. 
The speci 
s of this situation are as follows: 
o An industry association recently held an informational 
conference. 
Association members 'vere charged $150. 
Industry representatives who were not members of the 
Association were charged $175.00. 
The conference fee 
covered conference room rental, speakers' honoraria, 
refreshments, Association expenses and "profit". 
o I attended the conference without charge. 
participation 
"vas part of my professional acti vi ties in that the 
information presented was relevant to legislative research 
I am conducting. 
I 
not participate in the conference 
as a speaker or any other type of "presenter". 
s 
travel costs to and 


reports that additional public or private officials would 
have been welcome to attend without charge, had they 
requested permission, as I had done. 
I do not feel that the waived conference fee should be a 
reportable gift for the following reasons: 
o I participated as part of my professional activities. 
o The conference was informational, not political. 
o Conference fees were waived for other governmental 
employees, and private industry representatives, indicating 
that I did not receive any "special" treatment unavailable 
to other public officials in similar positions. 
o The passage of Proposition 24 has resulted in the freezina 
of staff travel budgets, thereby necessitating that staff 
seek means to cut travel costs to fulfill pro 
ssioanl 
responsibilities. 
I hope that you will agree that the circumstances outlined 
above do not warrant this fee-waiver to be considered a 
reportable gift. 
However, if the Commission determines that this 
is a gift, I have the following additional question relating to 
how much I need to report: 
o Is the "profit" portion of the fee (that is, the amount of 
the fee collected by the Association above its expenses) 
part of the gift? 
I feel that the "profit" should not be 
part of the gift because the "profit" is intended to be 
made from industry attendees, not governmental 
participants. 
Thank you for your prompt attention to this matter. 
I stand 
ready to properly report this transaction once the Commission 
clarifies my responsibilities. 
IN:j 
cc: 
Honorable Sam Farr 
Doug Kim 
Deputy Administrative Officer 
s 
Sincerely, 
Associate Consultant

executive/legal

322-6 .... 1 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political 
Reform Act has been referred to Janis Shank McLean, an 
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at 
(916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex 
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to 
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21 
working days. 
Very truly yours, 
General Counsel 
BAM:plh

Commlftee Secretary 
At the suggestion of Carla Wardlow of your Technical 
Assistance Division, I am writing for clarification of whether 
the waiver of a conference fee constitutes a reportable "gift" 
under the Fair Political Practices Act. 
The specifics of this situation are as follows: 
o An industry association recently held an informational 
conference. 
Association members were charged $150. 
Industry representatives who were not members of the 
Association were charged $175.00. 
The conference fee 
covered conference room rental, speakers' honoraria, 
refreshments, Association expenses and "profit". 
o I attended the conference without charge. 
My participation 
was part of my professional activities in that the 
information presented was relevant to legislative research 
I am conducting. 
I did not participate in the conference 
as a speaker or any other type of "presenter". 
o I paid out of personal funds my travel costs to and from 
the conference. 
o At least one other legislative employee (from a different 
office), and two other private industry representatives, 
also attended the conference as passive participants 
without charge. 
An industry association representative 


.-. 
reports that additional public or private officials would 
have been welcome to attend without charge, had they 
requested permission, as I had done. 
I do not fee~ that the waived conference fee should be a 
reportable gift for the following reasons: 
o I participated as part of my professional activities. 
o The conference was informational, not political. 
o Conference fees were waived for other governmental 
employees, and private industry representatives, indicating 
that I did not receive any "special" treatment unavailable 
to other public officials in similar positions. 
o The passage of Proposition 24 has resulted in the freezing 
of staff travel budgets, thereby necessitating that staff 
seek means to cut travel costs to fulfill professioanl 
responsibilities. 
I hope that you will agree that the circumstances outlined 
above do not warrant this fee-waiver to be considered a 
reportable gift. 
However, if the Commission determines that this 
is a gift, I have the following additional question relating to 
how much I need to report: 
o Is the "profit" portion of the fee (that is, the amount of 
the fee collected by the Association above its expenses) 
part of the gift? 
I feel that the "profit" should not be 
part of the gift because the "profit" is intended to be 
made from industry attendees, not governmental 
participants. 
Thank you for your prompt attention to this matter. 
I stand 
ready to properly report this transaction once the Commission 
clarifies my responsibilities.
